title: Wave-particle duality of unpolarized photons
abstract: Photons in a two-path interferometer best embody wave-particle duality (WPD), which is a core concept of quantum theory. So far, the WPD relation is commonly written as $V^2+D^2 \leq 1$, where $V$ is the interference fringe visibility and $D$ is path distinguishability, i.e., the distinguishability of which path a photon passed. This inequality is saturated only when the which-way marker (WWM), which embodies which-path information (WPI) via an internal degree of freedom of photons, such as polarization, is in a pure state. For mixed-state WWM, conventionally defined distinguishability underestimates the amount of WPI and thus does not saturate the WPD relation. Here, we introduce a generalized measure of distinguishability $D$ that properly quantifies the WPI and saturates the WPD relation for all pure- and mixed-state WWM within a purification-based framework. To this end, mixed-state WWM is treated as a result of entanglement formation between the WWM and an external degree of freedom, e.g., environment, and $D$ is defined so that it incorporates the total WPI shared between the WWM and the environment. We show that $D$ thus defined is experimentally quantifiable, independently of $V$, without access to the environment. We experimentally evaluate $V$ and $D$ using true single photons generated in the completely mixed (unpolarized) state, and thus verify the saturated WPD relation.
summary: This paper addresses the inaccuracy of the wave-particle duality relation when dealing with mixed-state quantum systems. By redefining distinguishability to include information shared with the environment, the authors demonstrate that the fundamental duality equality can be restored even for unpolarized photons. The findings were experimentally verified using single photons from an NV center in diamond.
main_problem: The conventional wave-particle duality (WPD) relation, V^2 + D^2 <= 1, fails to saturate for mixed-state which-way markers because the standard definition of distinguishability underestimates the total which-path information.
main_result: The authors introduce a generalized measure of distinguishability (D) that incorporates information shared with the environment, successfully restoring the saturated WPD equality (V^2 + D^2 = 1) for unpolarized photons.
method: The study uses a purification-based framework to model mixed states and proposes an experimental method to quantify the new D using projective measurements on the which-way marker.
model_or_system: A Michelson interferometer using single photons in a completely mixed (unpolarized) state, generated from a nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center in diamond, where photon polarization serves as the which-way marker.
key_observables: Interference fringe visibility (V), generalized distinguishability (D), conventional distinguishability (Dc), polarization coherence, and the likelihood of a which-way guess (L).
important_parameters: Unpolarized (completely mixed) polarization state, coherence length of 13.5 micrometers, and the rotation angles of waveplates used to induce which-path information.
main_assumptions: The definition of D is primarily demonstrated for qubit-based systems (polarization); higher-dimensional generalizations are theoretically possible but computationally difficult.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the Michelson interferometer setup; Figure 3 displays interference fringes showing intrinsic coherence, the effect of which-path information, and the revival of fringes via quantum erasure.
paper_structure: The paper begins by identifying the failure of the standard WPD relation for mixed states, introduces a theoretical framework for a generalized distinguishability D using purification, describes the experimental setup with NV center photons, and concludes with experimental verification of the saturated WPD relation.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in quantum optics and open quantum systems as it provides a fundamental correction to how complementarity and information retrieval are quantified when a system is entangled with its environment.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Michelson interferometer setup that we used in our experiments. PMSMF: a polarization-maintaining single-mode fiber, NPBS: a non-polarizing beamsplitter, RR: retroreflectors, QWP(0–3): quarter-wave plates, HWP(0–1): half-wave plates, PBS: polarizing beamsplitters, APD(00–11): avalanche photodiodes. The green shaded areas represent the polarization-analyzing detectors used for the quantum erasure setup and the measurement of the path distinguishability. See the text for details.

Fig. 2. The visibility 𝑉, the conventional path distinguishability 𝐷c, the redefined path distinguishability 𝐷, and 𝑉2 + 𝐷2 are depicted as functions of 𝜃1 (the fast axis angle of QWP1 in Fig. 1), which varies the WPI. Their behaviors are classified into six cases, (a)–(f), as described in the text.

Fig. 3. Interference fringes (𝐺(1)) observed in the experiments using unpolarized single photons. (a) 𝐺(1) observed at OUT1 without QWP0 and QWP1. (b) 𝐺(1) observed at OUT0 (yellow) and OUT1 (purple) when WPI is completely absent (𝜃0 = 𝜃1 = 0◦). (c) Same as (b) but observed at APD10 (blue) and APD11 (red), which detect photons of OUT1 on the circular polarization bases. (d) 𝐺(1) observed at OUT0 (yellow), OUT1 (purple), APD10 (blue), and APD11 (red) when maximum WPI is added (𝜃0 = 0◦and 𝜃1 = 45◦).

Fig. 4. Comparison of the measured and theoretical values of the visibility 𝑉(blue), the redefined path distinguishability 𝐷(red), and 𝑉2 + 𝐷2 (purple) as functions of 𝜃1 when 𝜃0 = 0◦. The markers indicate the measured data, which are accompanied by the error bars that indicate 95% confidence intervals. The solid curves indicate the ideal theoretical values. The dashed curves indicate the theoretical values taking account of the experimental imperfections (see text for details).
